6. Optical Flow

Horn & Schunck 1981

Downstream tasks of optical flow

  • Video editing, slow motion (frame interpolation), video super resolution, in-painting, special effects, video stabilization
  • Action recognition, object tracking and surveillance
  • Egomotion, SLAM
  • Connection to video compression
  • Medical imaging: very similar to dense correspondences
  • Optical mice
  • Non-destructive testing, analysis of machinery, physics (e.g. Fluid flow)
  • Generative video??
  • Biological cognition understanding, time to contact (looming)

Summary of techniques: https://cs.brown.edu/people/dqsun/pubs/cvpr_2010_flow.pdf

6.1. Classical Eulerian approach

Color constancy assumption (CCA): A pixel with intensity has moved by during timestep without changing intensity. We linearize the problem via Taylor expansion:

(6.1)

where is the velocity . This gives us an energy minimization problem to compute the flow between two tiemsteps.

(6.2)

We add a regularizer for smoothness and to break degeneracy (for each pixel we have2 variables 1 equation, called aperture problem9) [TODO] PDE solution existence and uniqueness?

Variants

  • Edge-aware regularization modifies the smooth flow prior so that varies in space and drops at image edges. (e.g. decreaseing function of image gradient magnitude)

  • Another way is to break degeneracy is to assume pixels in the same patch generally share the same motion (Lukas Kanade).

    (6.3)

    for every pixel in a window surrounding the current pixel.

  • You can couple across time with a regularizer to ensure the flow varies smoothly in time at each pixel.
  • You can improve estimation via course-to-fine iterative estimation with pyramids.

Remark. If you estimate flow over multiple consecutive frames and integrate it, you get long-term drift due to accumulating errors.

[TODO] The problem is Langrangian in nature so where is the Lagrangian approach.

6.2. Supervised optical flow

FlowNet (2015): Single feedforward applied to stacked pair of frames (6-channel tensor). Refinement step at the end upsamples and computes a prediction (2-channel tensor). They created synthetic data on flying chairs lol. Can add correlation volume10 as an additional feature

RAFT: Recurrent All-pairs Field Transforms

  1. A “feature” encoder maps each image to a dense feature map at 1/8 resolution. A separate “context” encoder runs on to produce per-pixel context features that condition the update rule.
  2. Compute correlations between every location in and every location in . Build a pyramid by pooling at multiple scales.

  3. Initialize flow to zero, and iterate:

    • For each pixel in image 1, use current flow to point to location in image 2, and compute correlation between and neighborhood aroudn .
    • Recurrent update network takes current flow estimate, sampled correlation features, context features, and outputs a flow increment.
    • Upsample predicted flow from 1/8 to full resolution via a per-pixel 3x3 weight distribution. In contrast, determinstic bilinear upsampling would compute the high-res value by converting it to fractional coordinate in low res and taking 4 nearest low-res pixels and outputting weighted average using weights proportional to distance.

The Surprising Effectiveness of Diffusion Models for Optical Flow and Monocular Depth Estimation. Condition a diffusion model to denoise based on the two input images.

6.3. Self-supervised optical flow

SMURF: Fine-tune an existing network like RAFT via reconstruction loss with smooth regularizers.

[TODO] FlowFormer:

  1. Extract features from the two frames.
  2. Build a big “cost volume” that, for each pixel in frame A, measures how well it matches many candidate pixels in frame B.
  3. Turn that cost volume into tokens and run transformer attention over them, so the model can reason globally about correspondences (not just locally).
  4. Decode those attended costs into a flow field (a 2D motion vector per pixel), often with iterative refinement.
